title: A New Approach to the Local Langlands Correspondence for $GL_n$ Over p-Adic Fields
speaker: Peter Scholze
abstract: We give a new local characterization of the Local Langlands Correspondence, using deformation spaces of p-divisible groups, and show its existence by a comparison with the cohomology of some Shimura varieties. This reproves results of Harris-Taylor on the compatibility of local and global correspondences, but completely avoids the use of Igusa varieties and instead relies on the classical method of counting points a la Langlands and Kottwitz.

proof_stages.label: Geometric target for local Langlands
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the local p-adic notation and sets the goal of realizing local Langlands for all irreducible representations of GL_n by constructing Bernstein-center elements from a moduli stack of one-dimensional p-divisible groups.

proof_stages.label: Local deformation tower and nearby cycles
proof_stages.summary: For an F_{p^r}-point represented by a p-divisible group, the Dieudonné-module parameter delta, its deformation space, level-p^m covers, and the resulting nearby-cycle cohomology with GL_n and Weil actions are constructed.

proof_stages.label: Trace functions and transfer
proof_stages.summary: After inserting a compact cutoff function h, the trace of tau on nearby cycles defines a function phi_{tau,h}; Illusie/Faltings deformation theory, algebraization, and independence of l show it is a rational locally constant function, whose transfer gives the desired local function on GL_n(F).

proof_stages.label: Trace characterization of the Weil representation
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that for every irreducible pi there is a unique n-dimensional Weil representation rec(pi) characterized by the identity tr(F_{tau,h}|pi)=tr(tau|rec(pi))tr(h|pi), with the expected normalization, parabolic-induction behavior, supercuspidal bijection, and compatibility properties.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-variety trace formula with local terms
proof_stages.summary: For suitable PEL Shimura varieties, the trace of tau h f^p on cohomology is moved to special fibers, evaluated by Lefschetz and Kottwitz fixed-point formulas, and rewritten as a sum of orbital integrals whose p-part is the same locally defined function phi_{tau,h}.

proof_stages.label: Harris–Taylor unitary comparison and local-global compatibility
proof_stages.summary: Specializing to the Harris–Taylor unitary group of signature (1,n−1) split at p, pseudo-stabilization, Matsushima’s formula, Clozel base change, and strong multiplicity one isolate a single automorphic representation and identify the local Weil representation in Shimura cohomology with rec(pi_p).

proof_stages.label: Supercuspidal bijectivity via inertia
proof_stages.summary: Using cyclic base change and local-global compatibility, the proof reduces bijectivity to the lemma that a representation whose attached Weil representation has inertia invariants is not supercuspidal; this lemma is proved by describing inertia-invariant nearby cycles through regular algebraizations, stratified special fibers, and purity.

examples.name: Lubin–Tate tower for supercuspidals
examples.note: The classical Lubin–Tate tower is recalled as the known geometric realization for supercuspidal representations, motivating the enlargement from formal groups to all one-dimensional p-divisible groups.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker goes beyond naming the Lubin–Tate tower by spelling out the deformation-space and level-structure tower construction, noting it is the usual Lubin–Tate-tower construction in the formal-group case

passes.reason: A standard Lubin–Tate deformation tower is used as the known supercuspidal realization, not as a failure case.
The Lubin–Tate tower is the basic/formal height-n endpoint in the one-dimensional p-divisible-group deformation picture, used positively for supercuspidals.

examples.name: Semistable-reduction comparison
examples.note: The speaker compares the stratified special fibers needed for inertia computations with semistable reduction, only to emphasize that the actual intersections and multiplicities can be worse.
examples.engagement_reason: semistable reduction is only invoked as an analogy, with the speaker saying the actual singularities can be worse rather than working through a semistable model

passes.reason: The worse-than-semistable special-fiber behavior is a limiting degeneration cited to rule out the naive semistable analogy.
The cited special-fiber geometry is a singular degeneration with nontransversal or high-multiplicity components, invoked to show the naive semistable-reduction analogy fails.
The comparison is used to emphasize worse-than-semistable behavior such as non-transversal intersections and high multiplicities, defeating the semistable-reduction intuition.
The special-fiber geometry is cited as potentially having nontransversal intersections and high multiplicities, a degenerate reduction phenomenon used to show the semistable-looking analogy can fail.
The bad special fibers are contrasted with semistable reduction, since nontransversal intersections and high multiplicities show the semistable analogy can fail.
Semistable reduction is invoked only as a rough standard comparison for the stratification, not as a specific failure example.
Invoked to show that the actual special fibers need not be semistable, since they can have non-transversal intersections and high multiplicities, i.e. a singular degeneration.
semistable reduction is invoked only as a comparison or foil for the actual geometry, not as a failure example or sporadic/extreme object.
the comparison is invoked to deny a semistable-reduction picture, since the relevant special fibers may have non-transversal intersections and high multiplicities
The semistable analogy is explicitly undercut by possible nontransversal intersections and high multiplicities, giving a degenerate bad-reduction obstruction to treating it as semistable.
the comparison is used to warn that the regular-strata condition need not give semistable reduction, since the special fiber may have nontransversal intersections and high multiplicities
the limiting special-fiber geometry is invoked to defeat the semistable-reduction intuition, since intersections may be nontransversal and components high-multiplicity

examples.name: Unramified PEL data at p
examples.note: Unramified PEL Shimura data are mentioned as a clean case where the required hyperspecial level and integral canonical model assumptions are satisfied.
examples.engagement_reason: unramified PEL data at p are cited as a case where the desired integral-model framework works, but no specific calculation or construction is carried out for that case
